sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=17, col=16).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 10, level 2.
Valid actions right now: MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental. You may also `predict(expect_change=..., expected_grid=...)` before an `action(...)` to test your world model; `last_prediction_result`/`prediction_stats` then score it, and a `surprise` flags where your model was wrong -- optional, use it when you hold a belief worth checking.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Level 2 has left gray panel (with dark square at rows 16-19) and right checkered panel (with 2 yellow objects). Below are 44 gray bars in grid patterns. Bottom has two boxes (blue with yellow dots, gray with yellow square) and blue blob.
- Plan: Try clicking the dark square in the left panel first - it's a unique interactive element. If that doesn't work, explore the bottom boxes.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 1 of about 72 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Clicking the dark square didn't change anything meaningful. The "g" node is actually a large background region (544 pixels), not just the small dark square I saw. The dark square at rows 16-19 is actually part of the charcoal (c) or dark gray (G) region.
Let me reconsider. Looking at the ASCII output again:
- Row 16: "GGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGccccGGGGGGB..." - the "cccc" is charcoal
- Row 17: "GGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGcccGGGGGGB..." - the "ccc" is charcoal
- Row 18: "GGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGcccGGGGGGB..." - the "ccc" is charcoal
- Row 19: "GGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGccccGGGGGGB..." - the "cccc" is charcoal
So the dark square is made of charcoal (c) pixels. Let me check if there's a separate node for it.
Actually, wait. The segmentation showed only 1 "g" node with 544 pixels, and it's the large background area. The dark square might be part of the charcoal nodes.
Let me think about this differently. The level has:
1. Left panel: gray background with a dark charcoal square
2. Right panel: checkered area with yellow objects
3. Below: gray bars in grids
4. Bottom: two boxes and blue blob
The dark square in the left panel might be a "hole" or "target" that needs to be filled. Or it might indicate something about which bars to remove.
Let me look at the bottom boxes more carefully. The left blue box has yellow dots, and the right gray box has a yellow square. These might be "keys" that need to be matched with something.
Actually, let me reconsider the whole puzzle. In level 1:
- I removed 6 gray bars
- I clicked the blue blob
- Level completed
In level 2, maybe I need to:
1. Remove specific gray bars (based on some pattern)
2. Click the blue blob
The question is: which bars to remove? Let me look at the yellow objects and bottom boxes for clues.
Looking at the bottom boxes:
- Left blue box: has yellow dots at specific positions
- Right gray box: has a yellow square
The yellow dots in the left blue box might indicate which bars to remove on the left side. The yellow square in the right gray box might indicate which bars to remove on the right side.
Let me look at the positions of the yellow dots in the left box more carefully. Node 110 has boundary [[57, 8], [57, 9], [59, 9], [59, 8]], which is a 2x2 or similar shape at rows 57-59, cols 8-9.
And node 109 has boundary [[55, 20], [55, 22], [56, 22], [56, 20]], which is at rows 55-56, cols 20-22.
Hmm, these positions don't directly map to bar positions. Let me think about this differently.
Actually, maybe the bottom boxes are "selectors" that determine which side's bars to remove. The left blue box might select the left side, and the right gray box might select the right side.
Or maybe the boxes show which bars are "active" - the yellow dots/squares indicate which bars need to be removed.
Let me try a different approach: look at the yellow objects in the checkered area and see if their positions correspond to bar positions.
The top yellow object (node 11) is at rows 7-11, cols 44-49. The bars on the right side are at cols 38-40, 43-45, 48-50, 53-55. The yellow object spans cols 44-49, which overlaps with bars at cols 43-45 and 48-50.
The bottom yellow object (node 44) is at rows 24-27, cols 45-48. This overlaps with bars at cols 43-45 and 48-50 as well.
Hmm, both yellow objects overlap with the same bar columns. Maybe I need to remove the bars that are "under" the yellow objects?
Actually, let me try clicking on one of the gray bars to see what happens. If clicking a bar removes it (like in level 1), then I can figure out the pattern from there.
Let me try clicking on a bar on the right side first, since the yellow objects are on the right side.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Clicking the dark square had no effect. The "g" node is the large background region, not just the small square. Need to figure out which bars to remove.
Plan: Try clicking one gray bar to test if bars are removable like in level 1. Start with a bar on the right side since yellow objects are there.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Let me check the bottom boxes more carefully
seg = current_frame.segmentation

# Find nodes in the bottom area (rows 50+)
print("=== Nodes in bottom area ===")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    b = n['boundary']
    min_row = min(p[0] for p in b)
    if min_row >= 50:
        print(f"  Node {n['id']}: color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={b}")

# Try clicking on a gray bar on the right side
# Node 61 is at row 33, cols 38-40
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 33, 'col': 39}])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
